step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for 15 years, about 10-15 cigarettes a day. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never lasts. I'm trying to quit again because I know it's bad for my health, but it's hard. I'm just really worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.
Therapist: I understand your concerns, Alex. It's great that you're aware of the risks and willing to try again. Can you tell me more about how you think quitting might affect your mental health?

Patient: Well, I've used cigarettes as a way to cope with my anxiety and depression in the past. When I'm feeling overwhelmed, a cigarette always seems to calm me down. I'm scared that if I quit, I'll just feel anxious and restless all the time. And what if it makes my depression worse? I don't know if I can handle that.

Therapist: I see. It sounds like you're worried about losing a coping mechanism that's been helpful to you in the past. That's a valid concern. But I want to assure you that we can work together to find new ways to manage your anxiety and depression that don't involve smoking. Would you be open to exploring some alternative coping strategies with me?

Patient: I guess so. But I'm not sure if anything will really work as well as cigarettes do. And what about the social aspect? I love smoking with my friends when we're out at parties. It's just something we do together. Quitting will make me feel like I'm missing out.

turns.user_message: Well, I've been getting winded easily lately, and I'm worried about the long-term effects of smoking on my lungs. And my doctor's been on my case about it, too. I guess I just don't want to be a smoker anymore.
step2_rag_chunks: Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.
Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: The patient has signaled they are ready to act or is wrapping up. Your ONLY job is to give ONE warm closing sentence. Do NOT ask any question. One sentence only. Nothing more.
